attributes.stem: A researcher wants to investigate the relationship between smoking status and respiratory symptoms in a community. They plan to survey 1,000 adults on a single day, collecting information about their current smoking habits and presence of respiratory symptoms.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the key characteristic of this cross-sectional study design?
options: Introduces an intervention to modify risk factors
Compares past exposures between cases and controls
Follows participants over time to track disease progression
Examines trends in disease occurrence over decades
attributes.explanation:
The key characteristic of a cross-sectional study is that it measures exposures and outcomes simultaneously at a single point in time. This makes it useful for determining disease prevalence and identifying potential associations, though it cannot establish temporal relationships or causation.

The incorrect options can be explained:

  • Introduces an intervention to modify risk factors describes an experimental study design where researchers actively test an intervention in a controlled setting.
  • Compares past exposures between cases and controls describes a case-control study design where researchers retrospectively examine exposure histories in people with and without a disease.
  • Follows participants over time to track disease progression describes a cohort study design where researchers monitor a group of participants prospectively to observe outcomes.
  • Examines trends in disease occurrence over decades describes an ecological study design where researchers analyse population-level data over extended periods.

Cross-sectional studies are efficient for describing the burden of disease in a population and generating hypotheses for future research, but their main limitation is the inability to determine whether exposure preceded outcome.
learningPoints: Cross-sectional studies measure exposures and outcomes at a single point in time in a defined population.
Cross-sectional studies are useful for determining disease prevalence but cannot establish causation.
Cross-sectional studies are relatively quick and inexpensive to conduct compared to other study designs.
references: Ray, Sumantra, and others (eds), Oxford Handbook of Clinical and Healthcare Research, 1, Oxford Medical Handbooks (Oxford, 2016; online edn, Oxford Academic, 1 Mar. 2016), https://doi.org/10.1093/med/9780199608478.001.0001, accessed 12 Dec. 2024.
